rooms.name: The Monarch Theatre — Grand Lobby
rooms.description: The grand lobby of the Monarch Theatre. A dusty chandelier hangs above cracked marble floors, and faded playbills line every wall. The brass ticket booth sits dark and empty. It smells like old velvet and forgotten glamour in here — the kind of place that used to matter to people. I can see why someone would fight to save it.

altBackgrounds.description: The lobby feels different now. The chandelier sways slightly — there's no draft. The playbills on the walls seem to watch me. After what I saw on that stage, every shadow in this building has a new meaning.

Vivian's chair by the ticket booth is empty. She's retreated to the dressing room — closer to Margaux's things, closer to the truth. The lobby feels abandoned now, like the theater is holding its breath.

hotspots.label: Ticket Booth
hotspots.description: The brass ticket booth — the glass is clouded with age, but I can make out a price card: 'Orchestra: $2.00 / Balcony: $0.75 / Standing: $0.25.' There's a stack of unsold tickets for the October 31st show inside. They never got to sell them. I wonder if people had already started staying away... if rumors about Margaux were circulating before that night.

hotspots.label: Concierge Desk
hotspots.description: An old concierge desk. There's a master key sitting on a brass hook and a note in shaky handwriting: 'Nancy — take the key. V.D.' Whoever wrote this was trembling. The ink smeared where the pen hesitated.

hotspots.label: Magnifying Glass
hotspots.description: A silver-handled magnifying glass. Vivian left another note: 'You'll need sharp eyes here.' She's been preparing for my visit. Almost like she's done this before — set up a trail of breadcrumbs for someone to follow.

hotspots.label: Chandelier
hotspots.description: A massive crystal chandelier — most of the prisms are dark, but a few still catch the light and scatter tiny rainbows across the marble. Hold on. One of the chain links is shinier than the rest. That's a recent repair. Someone's been maintaining this chandelier. In a building that's supposed to be condemned? That doesn't add up.

hotspots.label: Grand Staircase
hotspots.description: An ornate staircase — the carpet is worn right down the center, but the brass handrails are recently polished. Whoever's been walking through this lobby has been taking care of the place. That's not demolition crew behavior.

hotspots.label: Wall Painting
hotspots.description: A large painting of a city street — probably this neighborhood, decades ago. Cobblestones, gas lamps, horse-drawn carriages passing a row of theaters with bright marquees. The Monarch is right in the center, its facade gleaming and new. This must be from the 1920s, when the theater district was alive. A small brass plate at the bottom reads: 'Grand Avenue, 1926 — Commissioned by the Fontaine Estate.' The Fontaines built more than a theater. They built a whole world around it. And Ashworth wants to tear it all down.

hotspots.label: Faded Poster
hotspots.description: A half-torn touring poster: 'BLACKMOOR PLAYERS present: The Penvellyn Curse — A Tale of Alchemy and Shadows.' There's pencil at the bottom: 'Brilliant. Almost too real. —M.F.' Margaux's handwriting. A play about alchemy and poison. Given what happened to Margaux, 'almost too real' hits differently. Next to it, a smaller bill: 'Mystico the Magnificent — One Night Only!' with a cartoon magician in a top hat. Cute.

hotspots.label: Marble Floor
hotspots.description: Black and white marble checkerboard — gorgeous, even cracked. There's a worn path from the front doors to the auditorium, which makes sense. But there's a second path, fainter, heading toward a corridor marked 'STAFF ONLY.' The dust is scuffed. Someone's been using this route regularly, and recently. I should find out where it leads.

hotspots.label: Guest Ledger
hotspots.description: The guest ledger is open to recent entries. Mostly Ashworth's contractors — plumbers, electricians, structural engineers. But one entry appears over and over, going back fifteen years: 'E. Hale — Research.' Every single week. The handwriting never changes. That's not dedication, that's obsession. Flipping to an older page, I spot signatures from a 1990s gala — 'F. & J. Hardy, Bayport' and 'S. Joon — still looking!!!' with three exclamation marks. That name sounds familiar somehow.

hotspots.label: Coat Check
hotspots.description: An old coat check — mothballs and empty hangers. Wait. There's a modern rain jacket on one hook. That doesn't belong here. The pockets have a granola bar wrapper and... a receipt from ChemSource Industrial Supply. Dated last week. Someone bought antimony potassium tartrate (pharmaceutical grade), glycol fog fluid, and UV-reactive paint. Paid cash — no card trail. But the account name on the receipt is 'Monarch Properties LLC.' That's not a person's name. That's a company. And it sounds an awful lot like a shell corporation someone would set up to buy a theater. Antimony is a poison. Glycol is for fog machines. UV paint makes things glow. Whoever bought this had a very specific plan — and a very specific building in mind.

hotspots.label: Wall Sconce
hotspots.description: An ornate wall sconce — brass, art deco style, shaped like a sunburst. One of the glass shades is missing. I reach up to touch it and the fixture shifts slightly. There's a gap in the wall behind it... a faint rectangular outline where a door used to be, plastered over. Someone sealed off a doorway. I wonder what's on the other side.

hotspots.label: Ceiling Stain
hotspots.description: A water stain spreading across the ceiling plaster. Most of the damage is old, but this one spot is fresh — and it's directly above where that sealed door used to be. That's not a coincidence. Something above this spot is active. Pipes? Or something else entirely?

rooms.name: The Monarch Theatre — Auditorium
rooms.description: The auditorium takes my breath away. Rows of velvet seats descend toward a grand stage, its crimson curtain drawn shut. The air is thick and still — like the room is holding its breath. A single ghost light burns at center stage. The silence in here is the kind you can feel pressing against your ears. Something creaks in the balcony above. I look up. Nothing. But the hair on my arms is standing straight up.

altBackgrounds.description: The auditorium looks the same, but it doesn't feel the same. The ghost light flickers where it was steady before. There's a faint haze near the stage — residual fog? Or just my imagination working overtime. The scent of old roses lingers. I keep glancing at the spot where she vanished.

Someone turned on the house lights. The auditorium looks smaller, more mundane — exposed pipes in the ceiling, worn patches on the velvet seats, dust motes floating in the fluorescent glare. Without the shadows, the magic is gone. But so is the fear.

hotspots.label: The Stage
hotspots.description: This is where it happened. Right here on this stage, Margaux Fontaine drank from a poisoned goblet and collapsed in front of a full house. October 31st, 1928. A single ghost light burns at center stage — theater people say it keeps spirits at peace. Standing here, looking out at all those empty seats... I'm not sure it's working.

hotspots.label: Ghost Light
hotspots.description: The ghost light — just a bare bulb on a metal stand. Every theater keeps one burning when the house is dark. Tradition, superstition, or just so nobody falls off the stage. This one flickers, though, and the wiring looks brand new. Someone replaced this recently. Whoever it is, they're taking the tradition seriously.

hotspots.label: Crimson Curtain
hotspots.description: The original crimson curtain — it must weigh a ton. Heavy velvet, gold tassels, still beautiful despite the age. There's a brass plaque at stage left: 'For Margaux — the last light of the Monarch.' I run my hand along the hem and... it's damp. That's fog machine residue. Someone's been running fog through here. Recently.

hotspots.label: Audience Seats
hotspots.description: Rows and rows of dusty velvet seats stretching back into shadow. I check a few seat pockets — old ticket stubs, a candy wrapper from a brand I've never heard of. But seat G-12 stands out: the cushion is worn flat, and there's a coffee ring on the armrest. Someone's been sitting here regularly. For a long time.

hotspots.label: Seat G-12
hotspots.description: This is Edwin's regular seat — G-12. There's a folded note in the pocket, his handwriting: 'Lighting cue for Act III, Scene 4 — crimson wash, moonlight blue, center spot, crimson wash. She would have been standing HERE when the lights changed.' An arrow points to center stage. He's reconstructing the exact moment Margaux died. That's... incredibly thorough. Or incredibly personal.

hotspots.label: 1928 Playbill
hotspots.description: Tucked in a seat pocket near the front — a 1928 playbill in perfect condition. The cast page lists Margaux Fontaine as Lady Ashford and Cecilia Drake as understudy. Someone circled Cecilia's name in red ink. The ink is old — this wasn't done recently. Whoever marked this suspected Cecilia decades ago. I should hold onto this.

hotspots.label: Orchestra Pit
hotspots.description: The orchestra pit — no instruments, just dust and silence. But wait. Footprints. Clear shoe prints in the dust, leading from the stage-right stairs to a panel in the wall. The panel has no visible handle, but the footprints stop right in front of it. Someone knows how to open that. I should look at this from a different angle.

hotspots.label: Balcony
hotspots.description: The upper balcony is lost in shadow. I can barely make out the silhouettes of empty seats up there. For a second I could swear I see someone sitting in the front row — a dark shape, perfectly still. I blink and it's gone. Just shadows. Probably. My heart is beating a little faster than I'd like to admit.

hotspots.label: Ceiling Mural
hotspots.description: A mural of the nine Muses on the domed ceiling. The artist gave Melpomene — the Muse of Tragedy — special attention. She holds a dagger in one hand and a goblet in the other. The goblet is painted in incredible detail, gold and crimson. A goblet. The same kind of prop that killed Margaux. Was the artist trying to tell us something, or am I reading too much into a ceiling?

hotspots.label: Stage Floor Marks
hotspots.description: Colored tape on the stage floor — blocking marks telling actors where to stand. Most are faded, decades old. But there's a fresh set in white tape, marking a very specific path from upstage center to... the trapdoor. Someone has been rehearsing. Not a play — an entrance. The ghost's entrance. This was planned.

hotspots.label: Stage Lights
hotspots.description: Old stage lights along the proscenium arch. A few are still glowing — just enough to cast long shadows across the empty seats. The warm light almost makes this place feel alive. Almost.

hotspots.label: Side Exit
hotspots.description: The EXIT sign above this door buzzes and flickers like it can't decide whether to stay on. Through the door, a narrow corridor leads to 'DRESSING ROOMS / PROJECTION BOOTH.' It smells like old wood and something floral — perfume? From 1928? No, that's impossible. But my nose disagrees.

hotspots.label: Crumpled Paper
hotspots.description: A crumpled note on the stage floor. I smooth it out: 'I know what you've been doing in the basement. Stop now or I go to the police. — S.M.' S.M. — Stella Morrow. So Stella knows about the basement activity. She's been watching whoever is down there. But she didn't go to the police, and she didn't tell me. Why protect someone she's threatening?

rooms.name: The Monarch Theatre — Backstage
rooms.description: Backstage is a maze of rigging ropes, costume racks, and stacked set pieces. It smells like sawdust and old paint. Somewhere in the shadows, something mechanical is humming — a low, steady pulse I can feel through the floorboards. A draft moves through here that shouldn't exist — the air shifts like someone just walked past me. I'm not alone back here.

altBackgrounds.description: The fog machine is still leaking. Thin wisps curl along the floor between the costume racks, making everything look like a half-remembered dream. The mechanical humming is louder now — someone turned it up, or it never turned off.

Stella's workstation is cleared out. Her clipboard is gone, her coffee mug, the lockbox — all of it. She left in a hurry. A single prop rose sits on her chair, like an apology. The backstage feels emptier without her sharp presence organizing the chaos.

hotspots.label: Work Table
hotspots.description: A heavy work table covered in organized chaos — old scripts, hand tools, reference photographs. The papers aren't random; they're sorted by date. Someone's been researching the theater's history right here at this table. The handwriting on the sticky notes is small and precise. I've seen writing like that before. There's a spiral notebook open to a page of absent-minded doodles — a theater mask, then a spotlight, then curtains, then a rose. They're drawn in a row, like a sequence. Stella's handwriting is underneath: 'Don't forget.' Forget what?

hotspots.label: Fog Machine
hotspots.description: An industrial fog machine — old, but someone's kept it in working order. The fluid reservoir is half-empty and the nozzle is still damp. I touch it. Wet. This was used within the last day or two. And the brand label has been scratched off. Why would someone hide what kind of fog machine they're using? Unless the fluid is... specialized.

hotspots.label: Notice Board
hotspots.description: Shelves of old stage manuals and equipment logs — most gathering dust. But one binder has been pulled out recently: 'MONARCH THEATRE — SPECIAL EFFECTS MANUAL, 1925.' I flip through it. Fog machines, trapdoors, hidden passages — this building was designed with secrets built into the walls. If someone studied this manual carefully enough, they could stage just about anything.

hotspots.label: Prop Table
hotspots.description: A cluttered prop table — dusty globe, trick daggers, a small stone gargoyle. The gargoyle has a tag: 'Blackmoor Manor set — 1932 touring production. DO NOT DISCARD.' I pick it up. It's heavier than a prop should be. There might be something inside this. I should come back to it.

hotspots.label: Bust & Props
hotspots.description: Rows of old costumes — velvet, silk, moth-eaten lace. But one white gown near the back makes the hair on my arms stand up. It's not dusty. The hem is damp. I check the label with slightly unsteady hands: 'Lady Ashford — Act III.' This is Margaux's costume from the night she died. And someone has been wearing it. Recently. The fabric has a faint chemical smell and there's fog machine residue along the hem — whoever's playing ghost isn't improvising. They've been rehearsing.

hotspots.label: Rigging Ropes
hotspots.description: Dozens of hemp ropes running up to the fly loft — each one moves a different piece of scenery. The counterweights are massive lead bricks on steel cables. Don't touch. But one rope catches my eye — it's new. Fresh hemp, not frayed like the others. The label reads 'TRAP CENTER' in neat, precise handwriting. Someone replaced this rope recently — and they labeled it like a professional. Or an obsessive.

hotspots.label: Basement Door
hotspots.description: The door swings open and cold air rushes up from below. A steep staircase descends into pitch darkness beneath the stage. I can hear something down there — a faint mechanical hum. My flashlight barely reaches the bottom. Well, Nancy... you didn't come here to stand at the top of stairs. Although, for the record, standing at the top of stairs is significantly less terrifying.

hotspots.label: Lighting Panel
hotspots.description: The main lighting panel — dozens of switches, each labeled with a show name and date. One bank is labeled 'CRIMSON VEIL — ACT III' in faded ink. These controlled the lights during the performance when Margaux died. I lean closer and notice something metallic wedged behind the panel, pushed into the wall. Hidden on purpose?

hotspots.label: Paint Corner
hotspots.description: Old scenic paint cans — mostly dried solid. But one can of luminous white is freshly opened. I know this stuff. It glows under UV light. Classic theater trick for making things — or people — glow in the dark. Perfect for making a ghost visible on a pitch-black stage.

hotspots.label: Call Board
hotspots.description: The stage manager's call board — layers of old notices going back decades. One recent note in Stella's handwriting stands out: 'INVENTORY DISCREPANCY — Missing: 3 Tiffany lamps, 1 silver candelabra, 2 gilt frames (est. value: $4,200). DO NOT DISCUSS.' She wrote 'do not discuss' and pinned it to a board? That's not how you keep secrets, Stella. Unless you want a record to cover yourself later. Pinned underneath, a faded flyer: 'KOKO KRINGLE — Official Candy of the Monarch Theatre. A Taste You Won't Forget!' There's a half-eaten wrapper in the trash. Some things never change.

hotspots.label: Stella's Lockbox
hotspots.description: A metal lockbox shoved under Stella's work station. Four rotating dials, each engraved with little theatrical symbols — masks, spotlights, curtains, roses, and more. She doesn't want anyone getting into this. I need to find the right combination of symbols. Knowing Stella, the answer is probably somewhere nearby.

hotspots.label: Table Lamp
hotspots.description: A warm oil lamp sitting on the work table, casting a golden glow across the scattered papers and tools. Its flame flickers with every draft that moves through the backstage.

rooms.name: Margaux's Dressing Room
rooms.description: Time stopped in this room in 1928. Margaux's vanity mirror is still surrounded by lightbulbs — half of them dead — reflecting dusty perfume bottles and a dried corsage. A velvet settee sits beneath moth-eaten costumes. It smells like old powder and roses. She never came back to clean up. She never came back at all. The lights flicker when I step inside — just once — and then hold steady. Probably the wiring. Probably.

altBackgrounds.description: Margaux's trunk sits open now, the locket gone from its velvet cradle. The room feels lighter — like something that was waiting was finally released. The vanity mirror catches a reflection of the open trunk and for a moment, in the dusty glass, I swear I see someone standing behind me. When I turn, there's no one there. Just the scent of roses, stronger than before.

The wax letters on the mirror are visible now — C.D. — shining in the vanity lights like an accusation that's waited nearly a century to be read. Margaux wrote her killer's initials on this mirror knowing she might not survive to say the name aloud. The room feels charged, electric. Someone's secret has been exposed.

The heating vent panel hangs open, revealing the dark mouth of the hidden passage behind the wall. Cool air breathes from the tunnel, carrying the smell of dust and old stone. This is how Edwin moved through the building unseen — from the basement to this room, ghosting through walls. The dressing room will never feel the same.

hotspots.label: Margaux's Vanity
hotspots.description: Margaux's vanity. Her name is still painted on the mirror in faded gold letters. The lightbulbs around the frame are mostly dead, but two still glow, warm and amber. There are dried roses, an empty perfume bottle, and a hairbrush with dark strands still caught in the bristles. Almost a hundred years, and her hair is still here. It feels wrong to touch anything. But I have to.

hotspots.label: Vanity Mirror
hotspots.description: I hold the magnifying glass up to the mirror and tilt it in the light. There — barely visible — letters traced in wax on the glass surface. My hands are shaking a little. Margaux wrote this. Nearly a hundred years ago, she sat right where I'm standing and wrote: 'C.D. brings the tea. C.D. changed the goblet. C.D. will be the death of me.' She knew. She knew who was killing her, and nobody listened.

hotspots.label: Vanity Drawer
hotspots.description: Inside the drawer: a leather-bound diary with M.F. embossed in gold on the cover.

hotspots.label: Perfume Bottles
hotspots.description: Three crystal perfume bottles. Two are empty. The third still has a few drops of amber liquid. I uncork it carefully and... that's not perfume. The smell is bitter, chemical, wrong. Someone stored something else in this bottle. Something they wanted to disguise. My detective instincts are screaming — I should not touch this with bare hands.

hotspots.label: Rose Vase
hotspots.description: A corsage of dried red roses preserved under glass. The card reads: 'Break a leg, darling — C.' Someone with the initial C sent this to Margaux on the night of the final performance. 'Break a leg.' Whoever C was, they were close to Margaux — close enough to be backstage that night. Close enough to have access to the props. 'Break a leg' feels like a cruel joke if C knew what was about to happen.

hotspots.label: Hanging Costumes
hotspots.description: Hooks for Margaux's costumes — most are moth-eaten relics. But one hook is empty. The label reads 'Lady Ashford — Act III, The Crimson Veil.' The costume from her death scene is missing. Someone took it from this room. Someone who wanted to become Margaux's ghost.

hotspots.label: Wall Photos
hotspots.description: Framed photographs of Margaux in her roles — she was beautiful. Commanding. In one, she stands with a younger woman. The inscription reads: 'With my dear C., who will one day outshine us all.' The glass over the younger woman's face is cracked. Deliberately. Someone punched this. Someone who knew what she did.

hotspots.label: Table Lamp
hotspots.description: An ornate table lamp casting warm amber light. Its shade is slightly scorched — a reminder of the gaslight era when backstage fires were common.

hotspots.label: Makeup Table
hotspots.description: Stage makeup tins, mostly dried solid. One compact still opens — and inside the lid, there's a tiny photograph. A handsome man, 1920s style. On the back: 'Always, J.' Margaux kept his picture hidden in her makeup. Private. Intimate. J. — I need to find out who this was to her.

hotspots.label: Vanity Stool
hotspots.description: A worn velvet stool, still dented from years of use. Margaux sat here before every performance. Right here, under these lights, putting on her makeup, looking at J.'s photograph. I sit down for a moment. The mirror reflects a tired detective where a brilliant actress used to be. We'd probably have a lot to talk about, Margaux. You had a poisoner and a bad understudy. I have a poisoner and no understudy at all.

hotspots.label: Locked Trunk
hotspots.description: An antique trunk with a combination lock. Three dials, each numbered 0–9. The trunk is engraved with Margaux's initials: M.F.

hotspots.label: Old Book
hotspots.description: Wedged behind the settee cushion — a leather-bound book: 'The Alchemist's Apprentice — A Study of Poisons and Their Remedies, 1890.' A bookplate reads: 'From the library of the Penvellyn Family, Blackmoor Manor.' I flip through dog-eared pages until I find it: a section on antimony compounds, annotated in Margaux's handwriting. 'These are my symptoms exactly.' She was researching her own poisoning. She figured it out, documented it, and still nobody helped her. That's heartbreaking.

hotspots.label: Hanging Dried Roses
hotspots.description: Bunches of dried roses hanging from the ceiling — a theater tradition for good luck. These have been here for decades, their petals dark and brittle.

hotspots.label: Theater Mask
hotspots.description: A porcelain theater mask — comedy faces outward, but I turn it over and find the tragedy side. Scratched into the inner rim: 'For M. — the greatest actress who ever lived. —J.' There's J. again. He gave her this. Comedy and tragedy in one — that's a love letter from someone who understood her. I'm starting to think J. might be the key to this whole story.

hotspots.label: Vivian's Archive Box
hotspots.description: A box tucked in the corner — Vivian's private archive. She said I could look through Margaux's personal effects. Inside: bundles of letters tied with ribbon, playbills annotated in Margaux's hand, and a sealed envelope marked 'For Vivian — if something happens to me.' It was never opened. Vivian couldn't bring herself to read it. Inside is a single page in Margaux's handwriting: 'I know what C. has been putting in my tea. I can taste it. But I won't stop performing — this role is mine and I will die on that stage before I give it up. If you're reading this, ma petite étoile, then I did.' The bravest and most heartbreaking thing I've ever read.

rooms.name: Projection Booth
rooms.description: A cramped booth above the auditorium, packed with old projectors and film canisters. Diego Reyes has wedged a writing desk between the equipment — papers and coffee cups everywhere. It's cozy in a chaotic way. He's made himself at home up here, which means he hears everything that happens in the theater below.

altBackgrounds.description: Diego has spread the annotated script across his desk, weighted down with film canisters. He's circled things, drawn arrows, stuck Post-it notes everywhere. The old projectors throw warm light across his scattered research. He's as deep into this mystery as I am.

hotspots.label: Old Projector
hotspots.description: A 1920s carbon arc projector — beautifully preserved. There's still a reel loaded: 'The Crimson Veil — Rehearsal Footage, 1928.' Rehearsal footage. Of the play where Margaux died. The film is too degraded to screen, but if I hold it up to the light, I might be able to make out individual frames.

hotspots.label: Film Frames
hotspots.description: I hold the magnifying glass to the film strip. I can just make out individual frames — four key moments from the rehearsal. But they're out of order. Someone spliced the film and rearranged the sequence. If I put them back in the right order, I might see exactly what happened on stage that night.

hotspots.label: Film Reels
hotspots.description: Pairs of large film reels mounted on the wall. The celluloid is degraded but the labels are still legible — newsreels and short features from the 1920s and 30s. One reel is labeled 'SHADOW RANCH RODEO FOOTAGE — 1932.' Another: 'WAVERLY ACADEMY SPRING RECITAL.' Someone collected films from all over.

hotspots.label: Booth Window
hotspots.description: Through the thick glass I can see the whole auditorium — stage, seats, balcony. From up here, the projectionist would have seen everything. Every entrance, every exit. Including someone coming up through a trapdoor. If anyone witnessed what happened in 1928, it would have been whoever sat in this chair.

hotspots.label: Writing Desk
hotspots.description: Diego's writing desk — coffee cups and scattered pages everywhere. He's writing a play called 'The Ghost of the Monarch.' About a theater haunted by a murdered actress. I read a few lines. It's good. It's also uncomfortably close to what's actually happening here. Does Diego have a good imagination, or does he know more than he's letting on?

hotspots.label: Annotated Script
hotspots.description: The original script of 'The Crimson Veil' — the annotations in the margins are in old red ink. Someone circled specific letters throughout the stage directions. That's deliberate. If I read them in order, they might spell something. I should take this with me.

hotspots.label: Circled Letters
hotspots.description: Now that I have the script, I can study the circled letters more carefully. Red ink circles specific letters in the Act III stage directions. If I read them in order, they spell something. Let me work through this.

hotspots.label: Film Canisters
hotspots.description: Film canisters on a shelf — old newsreels, short features. One is labeled 'PRIVATE — DO NOT SCREEN.' Naturally, I open it. No film inside. Instead: a bundle of newspaper clippings about Margaux's death from different papers. The details contradict each other — one says 'accidental poisoning,' another says 'prop malfunction,' a third says 'under investigation.' Someone was collecting evidence that the official story didn't hold up.

hotspots.label: Desk Lamp
hotspots.description: A small desk lamp illuminating the cluttered work surface. Under its warm glow, scattered papers and notes are visible.

hotspots.label: Old Stool
hotspots.description: A worn wooden stool where the projectionist once sat for hours. Decades of splinters and coffee rings mark its surface.

hotspots.label: Decoded Film
hotspots.description: Now that the frames are in order, the sequence is devastating. Frame one: a toast on stage, the goblet is raised. Frame two: Margaux drinks. Frame three: she collapses. Frame four: a figure in the wings steps forward — the understudy, already in costume, already ready. She was prepared. She knew what was going to happen. This isn't a rehearsal gone wrong. It's a murder caught on film.

hotspots.label: Wall Scratches
hotspots.description: Decades of scratched graffiti from bored projectionists — initials, dates, little drawings. One makes me stop: 'M.F. + J.H. 1927 ♥.' Margaux Fontaine and J.H. — so J.'s last name started with H. And below it, much newer: 'E.H. was here. For her.' E.H. — someone with the same last initial as J.H. That's a coincidence I'm not comfortable ignoring. In the corner, scratched with what looks like a key: 'SONNY JOON WAS HERE — THE TRUTH IS OUT THERE' surrounded by tiny alien doodles. Okay, that one I can't explain.

rooms.name: Manager's Office
rooms.description: Ashworth's temporary headquarters — a modern laptop on an ornate mahogany desk, demolition blueprints pinned everywhere. There's a cot in the corner with rumpled sheets. He's been sleeping here since the poisoning. He looks pale and unwell, and the room has that stale, closed-in smell of someone who's afraid to leave.

altBackgrounds.description: The office feels different without Ashworth looming in it. His cot is a mess of tangled sheets — he left in a hurry. The desk is still buried in papers, the laptop still open, but the man himself is gone. The room smells like cold coffee and fear. Whatever he's running from, it isn't just me.

hotspots.label: Mahogany Desk
hotspots.description: A gorgeous mahogany desk buried under papers — contracts, demolition permits, insurance forms. Ashworth has been busy. I glance at the top document: a liability waiver for the demolition crew. He's already planning the teardown while people are still living in this building. Nothing says 'get well soon' like scheduling the destruction of the building you were poisoned in. Priorities.

hotspots.label: Blueprints
hotspots.description: Demolition blueprints for the Monarch. But someone has drawn all over them in red pen — hidden passages, sealed rooms, a tunnel network beneath the stage. This isn't demolition planning. This is exploration. And the handwriting... small, precise, meticulous. That's Edwin's handwriting. What are Edwin's notes doing on Ashworth's blueprints?

hotspots.label: Telephone
hotspots.description: An old rotary phone on the desk corner — still connected, faint dial tone. The last number dialed is on a sticky note: a demolition contractor. Even from his sickbed, Ashworth is trying to push the demolition forward. The man is nothing if not determined.

hotspots.label: Teacup
hotspots.description: A porcelain teacup with dried residue at the bottom. I lean close — there's a faint metallic smell. Whatever Ashworth drank, the evidence is still right here. I should take this. Carefully.

hotspots.label: Laptop
hotspots.description: Ashworth's laptop, open to an email chain. The latest: 'The historical society's petition is meaningless. Proceed with demolition on schedule.' Cold. But his unsent reply draft is more interesting: 'There's someone in the building at night. I have proof.' He never sent it. Why not? Did something stop him? Or did something happen to him before he could hit send?

hotspots.label: Filing Cabinet
hotspots.description: A metal filing cabinet. Most drawers are demolition permits and contractor invoices. But the bottom drawer — jackpot. A folder labeled 'HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE ASSESSMENT — MONARCH THEATRE' from the city's heritage department. Status: 'Pending Review.' Someone filed for landmark status. And Ashworth buried it in his own filing cabinet. He's racing to demolish before this review goes through. That's not illegal, but it's not exactly ethical either.

hotspots.label: Painting
hotspots.description: A painting of a rain-slicked city street at dusk — gas lamps glowing along the sidewalk, the Monarch Theatre's marquee bright in the distance. It looks like the view from this very window, decades ago. A small brass plaque reads: 'Grand Avenue, 1924.' Ashworth hung a picture of the neighborhood he's about to demolish right above his desk. Either he doesn't notice the irony, or he enjoys it.

hotspots.label: Wall Safe
hotspots.description: A modern wall safe behind a painting of the theater. It has an electronic keypad.

hotspots.label: Office Window
hotspots.description: The office window overlooks the alley behind the theater. Rain streaks down the glass, but I can see clearly enough — there's a service entrance to the basement down there, and it's propped open with a brick. Someone's been coming and going without using the main entrance. That's how they avoid being seen. I need to find my way down there.

hotspots.label: Desk Lamp
hotspots.description: A green banker's lamp casting warm light across the desk. Its chain pull glints in the dim room.

hotspots.label: Open Drawer
hotspots.description: A desk drawer left slightly open. Inside: a flashlight and a book — 'Ghosts of the American Theatre.' The chapter on the Monarch is bookmarked, with passages underlined in shaky pen. Ashworth was researching the ghost. The tough businessman who calls everyone a 'vulture' and threatens lawsuits before breakfast was reading ghost stories with the lights on. He's more frightened than he'd ever admit. Join the club, Ashworth.

hotspots.label: Pinned Notes
hotspots.description: A printed demolition timeline pinned to the wall. Day 1 — Final inspection. Day 2 — Utilities disconnected. Day 3 — Demolition begins, east wing. Today is circled: Day 1. Two days. That's all this theater has left. Two days to solve a century-old murder and a brand-new poisoning. No pressure, Drew.

hotspots.label: Poisoned Teacup
hotspots.description: The teacup from Ashworth's poisoning — still sitting on his desk with crystalline residue at the bottom. The police left it here as 'not evidence enough.' But that alchemy book from the dressing room has detailed annotations about compounds and their effects. If I compare the residue to what's described in those notes, I can identify exactly what was used.

hotspots.label: Ashworth's Laptop (Quick!)
hotspots.description: His email is open. I scroll fast — there. An email to his lawyer: 'File the condemnation order BEFORE the historical review goes through. If they grant landmark status first, we lose everything.' And below that, a draft to the insurance adjuster: 'The hazard clause triggers upon confirmed toxic contamination of the premises. Proceed with expedited assessment.' He was planning the condemnation timeline around the poisoning. Before it happened.

hotspots.label: Wastebasket (Quick!)
hotspots.description: A crumpled receipt from ChemSource Industrial Supply. The order: antimony potassium tartrate, 25g, pharmaceutical grade. Paid cash. The shipping address is a P.O. box, but the account name — 'Monarch Properties LLC' — that's Ashworth's shell company. The same one on the coat check receipt. He ordered the poison himself.

hotspots.label: Under the Pillow (Quick!)
hotspots.description: A prepaid burner phone wedged under Ashworth's pillow. One contact: 'M. Lawson.' Three outgoing calls in the last week, all after 11 PM. The last text message sent: 'Contamination confirmed. File hazard assessment Monday.' That was sent the night BEFORE the police confirmed the poisoning. He knew what the test results would show because he already knew what was in the tea.

hotspots.label: Ashworth's Cot
hotspots.description: A military-style cot with tangled sheets and a dented pillow. He wasn't just working late — he was living here. The sheets are still warm-ish. A bottle of antacid tablets is wedged between the mattress and frame, and there's a half-eaten sleeve of crackers on the floor beside it. He left in a hurry. Didn't even grab his coat. Whatever spooked him, it happened fast.

hotspots.label: Pillow
hotspots.description: The pillow still has the impression of his head in it. There's a folded piece of paper underneath — a handwritten note in shaky block letters: 'I KNOW WHAT YOU'RE REALLY DOING. THE DEMOLITION. THE INSURANCE. ALL OF IT. STOP NOW.' Someone was threatening Ashworth — and they knew about the insurance scheme. The handwriting is shaky but deliberate. Judging by the sleeping arrangement, the threat was working. Ashworth was scared enough to sleep on top of it.

hotspots.label: Wall of Documents
hotspots.description: The wall is plastered with papers — more than before. Now that I know about the insurance payout, I can read between the lines. Demolition timeline, contractor bids, insurance rider amendments... and tucked in the corner, a handwritten list of names. Everyone in the theater, with notes next to each one. 'Vivian — sentimental, won't leave willingly.' 'Edwin — knows too much about the building.' 'Stella — useful, has keys.' He was profiling all of us.

hotspots.label: Filing Cabinets
hotspots.description: The filing cabinets from this angle — I can see one drawer left hanging open. He pulled files before he left. Whatever he took, he didn't want me finding it. But he missed one — a manila folder wedged behind the drawer. Inside: correspondence with an insurance adjuster. They were negotiating the payout BEFORE the poisoning happened. Ashworth didn't just benefit from the poisoning — he was planning around it.

hotspots.label: Scattered Files
hotspots.description: Stacks of files and loose papers scattered across the floor — some fell when he yanked open the cabinets, others look deliberately dropped. One catches my eye: a building inspector's report flagged 'URGENT.' The inspector found structural concerns that would delay demolition by months. This report is dated two weeks ago. Ashworth never filed it. He buried it. If the city saw this, the demolition would be on hold indefinitely.

hotspots.label: Theater Painting
hotspots.description: The painting of Grand Avenue, 1924. From this angle I notice something I missed before — the frame is slightly crooked. He moved it recently. The wall safe behind it is hanging open... and empty. Whatever was inside, Ashworth took it with him when he ran.

hotspots.label: Laptop (Unlocked)
hotspots.description: He left his laptop open. Without Ashworth hovering over my shoulder, I can read everything. The email to his lawyer is damning: 'File the condemnation order BEFORE the historical review. If they grant landmark status, we lose the insurance payout and the demolition timeline.' Below that, a sent email to 'M. Lawson, Hazard Assessment Services': 'Expect confirmed toxic contamination results within 48 hours. Begin paperwork for expedited condemnation.' That email was sent THREE DAYS before the poisoning. He wasn't predicting the future. He was planning it.

rooms.name: The Catwalk
rooms.description: Oh, this is high. A narrow metal walkway suspended above the stage — the grating flexes under my feet and I can see straight down through it. Lighting rigs hang at eye level. The ghost light flickers far below like a distant star. Every step I take echoes off the walls. Don't look down, Nancy. Don't look down.

altBackgrounds.description: The lighting board is still warm from the sequence I triggered. Colors wash across the stage below in slow cycles — red, blue, white — like the theater remembering its final performance. Edwin's notebook is gone from its hiding place. The catwalk feels less threatening now. Just high.

hotspots.label: Lighting Rig
hotspots.description: A bank of spotlights — some look original, 1920s hardware. One has been recently adjusted, its gel filter swapped for crimson red. I follow the beam angle down. It's aimed right at center stage. Exactly where Margaux would have stood in the final scene. Someone set this up to recreate that moment. That's commitment.

hotspots.label: Spotlights
hotspots.description: Large theatrical spotlights casting beams down through the dusty air. The light catches particles floating in the gloom.

hotspots.label: Metal Railing
hotspots.description: I grip the metal railing and immediately regret looking down. The stage is so far below — the ghost light is just a tiny warm dot in a sea of darkness. My stomach does a flip. Heights have never been my favorite. The catwalk sways slightly — just the wind, just the building settling — but my knuckles go white on the rail. Someone's been up here regularly, working with these lights. In the dark. Alone. I'd like to have a word with that person about their life choices.

hotspots.label: Lighting Board
hotspots.description: A lighting control board bolted to the catwalk. Four colored switches: Red, Blue, White, Red. There's a sequence panel — you can program lighting cues with it. I notice the board is slightly loose on one side. There might be something behind it. If I enter the right sequence, I bet I can find out.

hotspots.label: Leather Notebook
hotspots.description: A leather notebook hidden behind the compartment. I open it and my breath catches. The first page: 'The Ghost Project — E.H.' Edwin Hale. Pages and pages of research — timelines, passage diagrams, lighting cues, costume notes. This isn't a casual hobby. This is a blueprint for haunting a theater. He planned everything.

hotspots.label: Look Down
hotspots.description: I force myself to look down. The stage stretches out far below, and from this angle I can see what I couldn't from the floor — scuff marks worn in a path between the trapdoor and stage left. Regular use. And that panel in the orchestra pit with no handle? From up here I can see hinges. It's a door. There's a whole secret route through this building that nobody told me about.

hotspots.label: Fly System
hotspots.description: The original counterweight fly system — ropes and pulleys for raising and lowering scenery. One line has been recently re-rigged with new rope. I check the sandbag counterweight. It's been adjusted for approximately 170 pounds. That's not scenery weight — that's the weight of a person. Someone rigged this to carry themselves.

hotspots.label: Safety Harness
hotspots.description: A modern climbing harness clipped to the railing. This isn't theater equipment — it's a rock climbing harness. Someone's been up here doing work that requires both hands free over a fifty-foot drop. That's either very brave or very desperate. Possibly both.

hotspots.label: Curtain Below
hotspots.description: From up here you can see the top of the crimson curtain. It's thick with dust except where someone has recently pushed it aside — near the trapdoor position.

hotspots.label: Carved Initials
hotspots.description: Initials carved into the railing — decades of stagehands. One set stops me cold: 'M.F. ♥ J.H. — 1927.' M.F. and J.H. came up here together, a hundred feet above the world. They carved their names where nobody would see. Margaux and her mysterious J. This is the most intimate thing I've found yet. Whoever J.H. was, this wasn't casual.

rooms.name: Beneath the Stage
rooms.description: The air changes the moment I step off the stairs — cold, damp, heavy. A cavernous space stretches out beneath the stage. Massive trapdoor mechanisms loom in the shadows and old set pieces lean against the walls like sleeping giants. Something drips in the dark. A pipe groans overhead. Every instinct I have is telling me to go back up those stairs. But in the far corner, a work lamp casts a warm circle of light over something that definitely wasn't here in 1928. My pulse quickens. I think I've found what I've been looking for.

altBackgrounds.description: Now that I know what to look for, I can see Edwin's presence everywhere. Fresh scratches on the trapdoor mechanism. A tidy stack of research binders by the work lamp. A thermos of tea — still warm. He was just here. He might still be close.

The hidden passage entrance gapes open in the far wall — a dark rectangle barely wide enough for a person. Cool air flows from it carrying the smell of plaster dust and age. The passage network is real, and it explains everything. How the 'ghost' appeared and vanished. How Edwin moved unseen.

Edwin's workshop is dark now. The work lamp is off, the research binders packed up. The fog machine sits silent, its controls cold. It's just a basement again — concrete and pipes and old machinery. The ghosts are gone. All of them.

hotspots.label: Workshop
hotspots.description: I stare at this for a full minute before I can process it. A complete workshop — historical documents pinned to a board, chemical bottles lined up neatly, a fog machine remote, a white costume on a dress form, a notebook of timing cues. Someone built an entire ghost down here. Planned it, rehearsed it, perfected it. This took months. This took obsession.

hotspots.label: Bottle Shelves
hotspots.description: Chemical bottles on a shelf — some labeled for the fog machine, but others are pharmaceutical compounds. I read the labels and my stomach drops. Antimony tartrate. The same compound used to poison people in the 1920s. And here it is, in a basement underneath the theater where someone was recently poisoned. A measuring syringe sits beside the bottle with careful dosage notes — whoever did this calculated a precise amount. Enough to make someone very sick, but not enough to kill. That's not rage. That's planning.

hotspots.label: Fog Machine Controls
hotspots.description: A remote control system wired to the fog machines upstairs. Modified so one person can operate everything — fog, lights, and trapdoor, all from down here. The timing sequences are labeled in careful handwriting: 'Entrance,' 'The Walk,' 'Vanish.' Three acts of a one-man show. The ghost was never supernatural. It was a performance.

hotspots.label: Trapdoor Mechanism
hotspots.description: The original trapdoor mechanism — recently oiled, silent as a whisper. I test the pulley. A person could ride this from the basement to center stage in seconds. Up through the floor, into the light, and back down before anyone realizes what happened. This is how the ghost appeared. This is how the ghost vanished. Elegant. Terrifying. And absolutely premeditated.

hotspots.label: Stone Archway
hotspots.description: A section of the basement wall has been carefully dismantled — the mortar here is different from the rest, newer somehow. Someone noticed that inconsistency and pried it open. Inside the alcove: letters tied with ribbon, and a small vial with crystalline residue. The letters are correspondence with a pharmacist, dated October 1928. Someone bricked up the evidence of their own crime in this wall, probably thinking no one would ever look. It stayed hidden for almost a century — until someone studying this building noticed the mortar didn't match.

hotspots.label: Steam Machinery
hotspots.description: Old boiler equipment — some of it still running, somehow. Steam hisses from joints and the air is surprisingly warm down here. The machinery hums with a low pulse I can feel in my chest. That's the sound Diego's been hearing at night. Not a ghost. Just old pipes and a boiler that refuses to die.

hotspots.label: Work Lamp
hotspots.description: A modern work lamp — the only real light down here. It's plugged into an extension cord that someone ran along the ceiling and back toward the stairs. A long-term setup. Whoever works down here planned to stay a while.

hotspots.label: Hidden Passage
hotspots.description: A narrow opening in the basement wall — the bricks here are older, part of the original construction. It leads into a dark network of passages behind the theater's walls. I found Edwin's blueprints earlier. If I trace the route correctly, I can figure out where this passage connects to above.

hotspots.label: Old Crates
hotspots.description: Old set pieces stacked against the walls — a castle turret, a forest, a drawing room. Decades of stories piled up like cordwood. One flat makes me pause: it's from The Crimson Veil. A painted parlor with a table set for tea. And there, on the painted table — a goblet. The goblet. Seeing it painted so casually on a set piece, knowing what it actually did... it's chilling.

hotspots.label: Hidden Passage
hotspots.description: A narrow passage in the wall — I can just fit through sideways. It connects to the hidden corridor network I saw on those blueprints. Through here, someone could move from the basement to the dressing room to the lobby without ever being seen. The perfect route for a ghost who's actually a man with a plan.

hotspots.label: Water Pipes
hotspots.description: Copper water pipes along the ceiling, sweating with condensation. One pipe has been tapped — a thin rubber hose feeds down to a basin near the workshop. Running water. Whoever set up down here wasn't visiting. They were living here. Eating, sleeping, working — all in this dark hole beneath the stage. That's... lonely.

hotspots.label: Overhead Light
hotspots.description: A single bare bulb hanging from the ceiling, casting harsh shadows across the stone walls. Its pull-chain dangles just within reach.

hotspots.label: Sleeping Bag
hotspots.description: A sleeping bag, a camp stove, canned food — all neatly organized. And a neat stack of books — every single one about Margaux Fontaine, the Monarch Theatre, or theatrical special effects. Alphabetized by author. Even living in a freezing basement eating cold soup and sleeping on concrete, whoever's been down here organizes their bibliography. I don't know if that's devotion or madness. Maybe there's no difference.

hotspots.label: Strange Device
hotspots.description: Something glows faintly blue in the corner. I step closer — it's a UV light source. The kind that makes luminescent paint glow in the dark. Combine this with a white costume and luminous paint, and you'd get something that doesn't just appear — it would glow. Otherworldly. Beautiful. Completely fake.

hotspots.label: Evidence Board
hotspots.description: A corkboard covered in photographs, documents, and notes connected by strings. It's Edwin's investigation — his own version of what I do. Fifteen years of research mapped out on a board. But it's incomplete. He has the 1928 timeline but not the modern connections. I can finish this. I can connect both timelines and prove the full truth.
